title: Mental Health — Suicide Risk, Crisis & Depression (Set 1)
subtitle: Suicide risk assessment, safety, crisis intervention & mood disorders
scenario: Assessing and protecting clients at risk for suicide, intervening in crisis, and caring for clients with depressive and mood disorders.

steps.q: A 29-year-old woman with severe major depressive disorder tells the technician she has saved her pills and plans to take them 'this weekend.' Which factor makes this the HIGHEST acute risk?

opts.text: A specific plan, available means, and a defined time frame
opts.rat: Ideation combined with a specific plan, accessible means, and a set time frame indicates high, imminent risk requiring immediate protection.

opts.text: She has a diagnosis of depression
opts.rat: A diagnosis alone does not establish imminent risk the way plan, means, and timeline do.

opts.text: She is a young adult
opts.rat: Age is one risk factor but is far less predictive than a specific plan with means and timing.

opts.text: She is currently hospitalized
opts.rat: Hospitalization is protective; it is not what makes the risk high.

steps.q: Asking a client directly, 'Are you thinking about killing yourself?' will most likely:

opts.text: Plant the idea and increase the risk
opts.rat: This is a myth; direct questioning does not create suicidal thoughts.

opts.text: Open honest discussion without increasing risk
opts.rat: Direct, matter-of-fact questioning conveys concern and allows accurate assessment; it does not increase risk.

opts.text: Cause the client to become violent
opts.rat: Asking about suicide does not provoke violence.

opts.text: Violate the client's confidentiality
opts.rat: Assessing safety is appropriate and within the technician's role.

steps.q: A client with active suicidal ideation and a plan is admitted. The technician's PRIORITY intervention is to:

opts.text: Have the client sign a no-suicide contract and continue routine rounds
opts.rat: No-suicide contracts do not ensure safety and cannot substitute for observation.

opts.text: Place the client in a private room alone to reduce stimulation
opts.rat: Isolating a suicidal client removes protective observation and increases risk.

opts.text: Initiate close/continuous observation and remove available means of self-harm
opts.rat: Protecting the client through close observation and removing means (belts, sharps, cords) is the immediate safety priority.

opts.text: Reassure the client that everything will be fine
opts.rat: False reassurance dismisses the client's distress and does not address safety.

steps.q: A severely depressed client who has been withdrawn suddenly appears calm and cheerful and begins giving away belongings. The technician should:

opts.text: Conclude the client has fully recovered
opts.rat: Sudden calm after severe depression can be an ominous sign, not proof of recovery.

opts.text: Reduce the level of observation as a reward
opts.rat: Lowering observation at this point could be dangerous.

opts.text: Encourage the client to give away more items
opts.rat: This ignores a potential warning sign.

opts.text: Recognize a possible warning sign of suicide and increase observation
opts.rat: A sudden lift in mood and giving away possessions may signal the client has decided to act on a plan; vigilance must increase.

steps.q: The single strongest predictor of future suicide risk is:

opts.text: A previous suicide attempt
opts.rat: A prior attempt is the strongest known predictor of future suicide; it must always be assessed and taken seriously.

opts.text: Being employed full-time
opts.rat: Employment is generally protective, not a strong risk predictor.

opts.text: Having many close friends
opts.rat: Strong social support is protective.

opts.text: A single episode of sadness
opts.rat: Transient sadness is far less predictive than a history of attempts.

steps.q: A client is placed on one-to-one suicide precautions. This means the technician must:

opts.text: Check the client every 30 minutes
opts.rat: Intermittent checks are a lower level of observation, not one-to-one.

opts.text: Maintain constant, arm's-length visual contact at all times, including toileting
opts.rat: One-to-one precautions require continuous, close visual observation at all times, including in the bathroom, to prevent self-harm.

opts.text: Observe the client only during the day shift
opts.rat: Continuous observation applies around the clock.

opts.text: Allow the client to be alone if calm
opts.rat: Constant observation cannot be interrupted based on apparent calm.

steps.q: To make the environment safe for a suicidal client, the technician should:

opts.text: Allow the client to keep all personal items unchecked
opts.rat: Unchecked belongings may contain means of self-harm.

opts.text: Provide a glass drinking cup and metal utensils
opts.rat: Glass and sharp utensils can be used for self-harm.

opts.text: Remove potentially harmful items such as belts, shoelaces, sharps, and cords
opts.rat: Removing means of self-harm from the environment is a core safety intervention for suicidal clients.

opts.text: Leave the client alone in a locked private room
opts.rat: Isolation removes observation and increases risk.

steps.q: A depressed client starting an antidepressant begins to have more energy but still voices hopelessness. This period is important because:

opts.text: The client is now at no risk
opts.rat: Increased energy with persistent hopelessness is a higher-risk period, not a safe one.

opts.text: Observation can safely be discontinued
opts.rat: This is precisely when vigilance should be maintained.

opts.text: The medication has clearly failed
opts.rat: Energy returning first is an expected pattern, not treatment failure.

opts.text: Suicide risk may increase when energy returns before mood improves
opts.rat: Returning energy can precede mood improvement, giving a still-hopeless client the ability to act on suicidal thoughts; observation must continue.

steps.q: A client says, 'Everyone would be better off without me.' The BEST response by the technician is to:

opts.text: Ask directly, 'Are you thinking about killing yourself?'
opts.rat: A statement implying worthlessness warrants a direct, caring suicide-risk assessment.

opts.text: Say, 'Don't talk like that.'
opts.rat: This shuts down communication and dismisses the client's feelings.

opts.text: Say, 'Everyone feels that way sometimes.'
opts.rat: This minimizes a potentially serious disclosure.

opts.text: Change the subject to something positive
opts.rat: Avoiding the statement neglects a possible safety concern.

steps.q: The MOST therapeutic response to a client who says, 'Nobody cares whether I live or die,' is:

opts.text: 'Of course people care about you.'
opts.rat: This is false reassurance that dismisses the feeling.

opts.text: 'You sound like you're feeling very alone right now.'
opts.rat: Reflecting the client's feeling acknowledges their experience and invites them to share more.

opts.text: 'Why would you say something like that?'
opts.rat: 'Why' questions can sound confrontational and block communication.

opts.text: 'You have so much to live for.'
opts.rat: This minimizes distress and is non-therapeutic.

steps.q: The focus of crisis intervention is BEST described as:

opts.text: Long-term personality change
opts.rat: Crisis intervention is short-term, not focused on restructuring personality.

opts.text: Exploring early childhood in depth
opts.rat: In-depth exploration is not the goal during an acute crisis.

opts.text: Short-term help focused on the immediate problem and coping/safety
opts.rat: Crisis intervention is time-limited and directed at resolving the immediate problem, restoring coping, and ensuring safety.

opts.text: Waiting for the crisis to resolve on its own
opts.rat: Active, supportive intervention is provided, not passive waiting.

steps.q: Which cluster of findings is most consistent with major depression?

opts.text: Elevated mood, decreased need for sleep, and rapid speech
opts.rat: These describe mania, not depression.

opts.text: Hallucinations and disorganized speech only
opts.rat: These are psychotic features, not the defining features of depression.

opts.text: Excessive goal-directed activity and grandiosity
opts.rat: These are manic symptoms.

opts.text: Depressed mood, loss of interest, appetite and sleep changes, and fatigue
opts.rat: Depressed mood, anhedonia, appetite/sleep disturbance, low energy, and worthlessness are core features of major depression.

steps.q: A client with severe depression has marked psychomotor retardation. The BEST approach to activities of daily living is to:

opts.text: Allow extra time with simple, step-by-step encouragement
opts.rat: Depression slows thinking and movement; extra time and gentle, structured guidance support functioning without frustration.

opts.text: Do everything for the client to conserve their energy
opts.rat: Doing everything fosters dependence and does not support recovery.

opts.text: Insist the client complete tasks quickly
opts.rat: Pressuring a slowed client increases frustration and failure.

opts.text: Withhold help entirely to force independence
opts.rat: Abruptly withholding support can overwhelm a severely depressed client.

steps.q: A withdrawn, depressed client refuses to get out of bed or attend meals. The technician should:

opts.text: Allow the client to remain isolated indefinitely
opts.rat: Prolonged isolation worsens depression.

opts.text: Provide consistent, gentle encouragement and structure for participation
opts.rat: Consistent, non-punitive encouragement with structure supports a withdrawn client without forcing or abandoning them.

opts.text: Demand immediate compliance
opts.rat: Demands are non-therapeutic and may increase resistance.

opts.text: Withdraw all attention until the client complies
opts.rat: Withdrawing attention is punitive and harmful.

steps.q: When teaching a client about a newly prescribed SSRI antidepressant, the technician should explain that:

opts.text: It works fully within 24 hours
opts.rat: Antidepressant effect is delayed, not immediate.

opts.text: It should be stopped as soon as mood improves
opts.rat: Stopping early risks relapse and discontinuation symptoms.

opts.text: Full effect may take 2–4 weeks or longer, and it should not be stopped abruptly
opts.rat: SSRIs have a delayed onset of 2–4+ weeks; clients should continue as prescribed and avoid abrupt discontinuation.

opts.text: It causes immediate dependence like a sedative
opts.rat: SSRIs do not cause the dependence associated with sedatives.

steps.q: A 20-year-old client is starting an antidepressant. The technician monitors closely because the black box warning notes an increased risk of:

opts.text: Immediate hair loss
opts.rat: This is not the black box concern.

opts.text: Permanent memory loss
opts.rat: This is not associated with SSRIs.

opts.text: Hyperthyroidism
opts.rat: This is unrelated to the warning.

opts.text: Suicidal thinking in those under 25, especially early in treatment
opts.rat: Antidepressants carry a black box warning for increased suicidality in children, adolescents, and young adults under 25, particularly when starting or changing the dose.

steps.q: A client grieving the recent death of a spouse says, 'I just can't believe he's gone.' The technician recognizes this as:

opts.text: A normal early grief reaction
opts.rat: Disbelief and denial are common, normal early responses in grief; supportive presence and listening are appropriate.

opts.text: A psychotic delusion requiring medication
opts.rat: Normal grief is not psychosis.

opts.text: Evidence of malingering
opts.rat: There is no basis to label normal grief as feigning.

opts.text: A sign the client needs restraint
opts.rat: Grief is not a reason for restraint.

steps.q: Which response by the technician is therapeutic for a grieving client?

opts.text: 'You need to move on with your life.'
opts.rat: This dismisses the client's grief and rushes the process.

opts.text: 'Tell me about your husband.'
opts.rat: Inviting the client to talk about the deceased facilitates healthy grieving and shows genuine interest.

opts.text: 'At least he is no longer suffering.'
opts.rat: This minimizes the loss and offers premature reassurance.

opts.text: 'You are still young and will find someone else.'
opts.rat: This dismisses the current loss.

steps.q: A woman 11 days postpartum reports hearing voices telling her the baby is 'evil.' The technician recognizes postpartum psychosis and knows the PRIORITY is to:

opts.text: Reassure her the thoughts will pass and let her rest with the baby
opts.rat: Postpartum psychosis is an emergency; leaving mother and infant together unsupervised is unsafe.

opts.text: Encourage more alone bonding time
opts.rat: Unsupervised time increases danger during postpartum psychosis.

opts.text: Ensure the safety of the mother and infant and never leave them alone together
opts.rat: Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency with real infant-harm risk; ensuring safety, direct supervision, and urgent escalation are the priority.

opts.text: Document the finding and reassess in the morning
opts.rat: This is an emergency requiring immediate action, not delayed reassessment.

steps.q: Which change suggests a depressed client is beginning to improve?

opts.text: Increasing social withdrawal
opts.rat: Greater withdrawal suggests worsening, not improvement.

opts.text: Refusing to speak with anyone
opts.rat: This indicates continued or worsening depression.

opts.text: Giving away personal possessions
opts.rat: This can be a warning sign of suicide, not improvement.

opts.text: Improved grooming, appetite, and participation in activities
opts.rat: Return of self-care, appetite, and engagement are signs of improving depression.

reference: Aligned to the California BVNPT Psychiatric Technician exam content area: Mental Health Care. Written from standard psychiatric-technician and nursing references. Pending faculty review.
